title: Sudan's Gum Arabic Sector Eyes Post-Crisis Revival as International Demand Surges
excerpt: Despite ongoing challenges, Sudanese exporters are leveraging new trade routes through Port Sudan to meet the global demand for gum arabic.
content:
PORT SUDAN — The gum arabic industry, long considered the backbone of Sudan’s agricultural exports, is undergoing a remarkable resilience-led recovery. Local exporters and farmers have established new logistical corridors to transport the valuable crop from the western Kordofan regions to the shipping docks of Port Sudan.

Global demand for gum arabic—an essential stabilizing agent used in soft drinks, confectionery, and pharmaceuticals—remains high. Sudan produces approximately 70% of the world’s supply, making it an indispensable player in the global food supply chain. High-quality acacia sap is harvested manually by farmers who have persisted despite the domestic crises.

International trading houses have recently established direct representation in Port Sudan, allowing for faster customs clearance and trade finance. 'The reopening of international trade lines has provided a lifeline to millions of families who depend on gum arabic harvesting,' said Ibrahim Nour, an agriculture economist in Port Sudan. With prices stabilizing on the international market, there is renewed hope that the sector can serve as a catalyst for wider economic recovery.
author: Zeinab Salih

title: Diplomatic Chiefs Agree to Framework for Port Sudan Logistics Corridor
excerpt: A high-level diplomatic summit has concluded with an agreement to streamline import-export procedures along the Red Sea coast.
content:
PORT SUDAN — Diplomatic envoys from Sudan, neighboring East African nations, and international trade partners have signed a landmark agreement to streamline the Port Sudan Logistics Corridor. The multilateral framework aims to cut customs red tape and facilitate the rapid movement of commercial cargo and humanitarian aid.

The Red Sea port has become the primary window to the world for Sudan. Under the new agreement, digital customs clearance systems will be synchronized, and security protocols will be standardized to reduce transit delays. Additionally, regional development banks have committed emergency funds to expand container handling capacity at the main docks.

Analysts view this agreement as a significant step toward stabilizing the regional economy. 'A fully functional, efficient port is vital not just for Sudan, but for landlocked neighbors who rely on our transit corridors,' remarked Hassan Al-Hadi, a senior trade policy advisor. Implementation is scheduled to begin within the next thirty days.
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: The Guardians of Meroe: Restoring the Pyramids of the Kingdom of Kush
excerpt: Sudanese archaeologists and international conservationists are working to preserve the ancient royal pyramids at Meroe from environmental degradation.
content:
MEROE — In the heart of Sudan's semi-desert landscape, a team of dedicated local archaeologists, supported by international conservation funds, has initiated an urgent stabilization project at the royal cemetery of Meroe. The pyramids, which date back to the Kingdom of Kush (circa 300 BC to 300 AD), are facing mounting threats from shifting desert sands and climate-induced wind erosion.

The restoration initiative focuses on reinforcing the sandstone masonry of the most fragile pyramids and creating natural windbreaks to redirect sand accumulation. Using traditional mortar formulas that match the original composition, the conservationists are repairing structural cracks while preserving the ancient inscriptions that detail Kushite royal lineages.

Beyond technical preservation, the project aims to train local youth in sustainable heritage management. 'Meroe is not just our past; it is a symbol of our national identity and resilience,' said Dr. Amna El-Sadiq, lead archaeologist. 'By involving local communities, we ensure that the guardians of this ancient legacy are the people who live beside it.'
author: Tariq Osman

title: Sudan's E-Learning Startups Pivot to Mobile Education Platforms
excerpt: With traditional schools facing disruptions, local technology startups are building low-bandwidth offline mobile learning systems.
content:
PORT SUDAN — As formal education faces unprecedented challenges across Sudan, a group of young tech entrepreneurs is stepping up to bridge the learning gap. Local startups are launching innovative e-learning applications designed to work on low-bandwidth networks and older-model smartphones, allowing thousands of displaced children to continue their primary and secondary education.

These platforms utilize SMS-based quizzes, compressed audio lectures, and offline cache systems that allow students to download lessons during brief periods of internet connectivity. Several initiatives have partnered with regional telecom operators to provide zero-rated data access to accredited educational portals.

'Education cannot wait for perfect conditions,' explained Fatima Al-Nour, co-founder of the EduSudan initiative. 'Our goal was to design a system that requires minimal data but delivers high-quality interactive lessons. The response from parents and volunteer teachers has been overwhelming.' Plans are underway to expand the curriculum to include vocational training courses by the end of the year.
author: Fatima Al-Nour

title: Al-Hilal Secures Crucial Victory in CAF Champions League Group Stage
excerpt: Omdurman's football giant Al-Hilal clinched a dramatic 2-1 win over West African rivals, boosting their continental playoff hopes.
content:
OMDURMAN — Sudan's premier football club, Al-Hilal, secured a vital three points in the CAF Champions League with a dramatic 2-1 victory over their opponents. The match, played in front of a passionate crowd, showcased the grit and determination of the Omdurman-based squad as they fight for a spot in the quarter-finals.

Al-Hilal opened the scoring in the 24th minute with a spectacular long-range strike from their star midfielder, sending the stadium into a frenzy. Although the visitors equalized early in the second half through a defensive lapse, Al-Hilal rallied back. The winning goal came in the 83rd minute via a clinical header from a corner kick.

'This victory is for the people of Omdurman and all of Sudan who support us through thick and thin,' said the head coach during his post-match conference. The win keeps Al-Hilal’s hopes of continental glory alive, setting up a highly anticipated clash with Egypt’s Al Ahly next month.
author: Hassan Bashir

title: Urgent Humanitarian Air Bridge Arrives in Port Sudan with Medical Supplies
excerpt: A cargo plane carrying vital medical supplies and surgical equipment landed in Port Sudan to replenish struggling regional clinics.
content:
PORT SUDAN — An international relief aircraft landed at Port Sudan International Airport carrying over 45 metric tons of emergency medical cargo. The shipment, coordinated by regional aid agencies and the Sudanese Red Crescent, includes specialized surgical kits, pediatric vaccines, and intensive care pharmaceuticals.

The supplies are earmarked for rapid distribution to local clinics and emergency response rooms in areas suffering from severe resource shortages. Over the past few months, local medical facilities have faced critical stockouts of basic antibiotics and surgical dressings.

'This air bridge is a critical intervention, but the need remains immense,' stated Yassir Abdullah, a representative of the coordinating medical relief committee. 'Our priority is to ensure these medical kits reach frontline health workers safely and without administrative delays.' Local emergency response groups are already mobilizing trucks to transport the cargo inland.
author: Yassir Abdullah

title: Khartoum's Historic Souq Omdurman Begins Gradual Rehabilitation
excerpt: Municipal committees are launching a phased clean-up and structural restoration of Sudan's largest traditional marketplace.
content:
OMDURMAN — Sudan’s largest traditional market, Souq Omdurman, is seeing its first coordinated rehabilitation efforts. Community-led municipal committees, working alongside local traders, have begun a phased cleanup and minor structural restoration to help vendors return to their shops.

The historic market, which spans several square kilometers and has stood for over a century, is a crucial economic hub. The initial phase of the project involves clearing debris, restoring basic solar-powered street lighting, and establishing community watch groups to secure stalls.

'Rebuilding Souq Omdurman is not just about commerce; it's about restoring the heartbeat of our community,' explained local merchant leader Salah Ahmed. Local trade guilds hope that the return of basic services will encourage wholesalers to resume operations, helping to lower food and commodity prices across Omdurman.
author: Amna El-Sadiq

title: Nile Floods Control System Gets Major Infrastructure Upgrade
excerpt: Engineers are reinforcing embankments and updating telemetry sensors along the Blue Nile ahead of the summer flood season.
content:
KHARTOUM — Technical teams from the Ministry of Irrigation and Water Resources have initiated a comprehensive upgrade of telemetry sensors and flood embankments along the Blue Nile. The project is designed to improve early warning systems and protect agricultural lands in the Gezira and Sennar regions from seasonal flooding.

The upgrade includes installing automated water-level sensors that transmit real-time data via satellite to a central monitoring unit. Additionally, heavy machinery is being deployed to reinforce weak points along the river banks that breached during previous heavy rainy seasons.

'Accurate, real-time data is our best defense against unpredictable river behavior,' said senior engineer Tariq Osman. 'These new sensors allow us to warn downstream agricultural schemes hours in advance, potentially saving crops worth millions of dollars.' The project is funded through a partnership with regional environmental organizations.
author: Tariq Osman

title: Decentralizing Healthcare: Mobile Clinics Reach Remote Gezira Villages
excerpt: A network of volunteer doctors is operating mobile medical vans to provide primary healthcare services to rural farming communities.
content:
GEZIRA STATE — In response to the closure of several regional hospitals, a network of volunteer Sudanese doctors has launched a mobile clinic initiative to serve rural farming communities in Gezira State. Operating out of retrofitted off-road utility vans, these mobile units bring primary healthcare, maternal checkups, and chronic disease management directly to remote villages.

Each clinic is staffed by a general practitioner, a pharmacist, and a laboratory technician. They carry basic diagnostic gear and a supply of essential medicines, which are distributed to patients free of charge. The initiative is funded by contributions from the Sudanese diaspora and local philanthropic organizations.

'Many of these families cannot afford the transport costs to seek medical care in major towns,' said Dr. Zeinab Salih, who coordinates the mobile network. 'By bringing the clinic to their doorstep, we prevent minor treatable infections from turning into life-threatening emergencies.' The group hopes to expand the fleet to cover neighboring Sennar State next month.
author: Zeinab Salih

title: Sudan's Fintech Sector Expands with Launch of Unified QR Code Payments
excerpt: A collaboration of local banks and tech startups has launched a national interoperable QR code standard to foster cashless trade.
content:
PORT SUDAN — Sudan's rapidly growing financial technology sector has achieved a major milestone with the launch of a unified, interoperable QR code payment system. The initiative, led by a coalition of local commercial banks and prominent software startups, aims to encourage cashless transactions among micro-merchants and street vendors.

Previously, digital wallet users could only transfer funds within the same network, limiting the utility of mobile money. The new standard allows any mobile banking application to read and process payments from any participating digital wallet, significantly reducing friction in everyday commerce.

'This is a game-changer for small traders who struggle to handle physical cash due to currency shortages,' noted fintech entrepreneur Mohamed El-Fatih. 'By lowering barriers to digital payments, we are bringing thousands of unbanked traders into the formal digital economy.' Several market syndicates in Port Sudan have already begun registering members for the QR system.
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: Security Council Authorizes New Mandate for Sudan Border Monitoring
excerpt: The UN Security Council has renewed its border monitoring mandate to prevent illicit trade and facilitate cross-border aid delivery.
content:
NEW YORK — In a unanimous vote, the United Nations Security Council has extended the mandate of its joint border monitoring mechanism for another year. The resolution emphasizes the critical role of international monitors in securing transit routes and ensuring that humanitarian aid corridors between Sudan and its neighbors remain open and demilitarized.

The renewed mandate calls for closer coordination with regional bodies, including the Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD) and the African Union. It also requests additional logistical support to monitor remote desert crossings where smuggling networks operate.

'Securing our international borders is paramount to restoring national stability and regional confidence,' said Sudan's diplomatic representative. The resolution also calls on all parties to respect international humanitarian law and ensure the safe passage of non-governmental aid convoys in border zones.
author: Yassir Abdullah

title: Sudan Central Bank Announces Measures to Stabilize Exchange Rate
excerpt: The central bank will auction foreign currency reserves and implement stricter controls on commercial exchange bureaus.
content:
PORT SUDAN — The Central Bank of Sudan has announced a series of emergency monetary interventions aimed at stabilizing the Sudanese Pound against major foreign currencies. The central bank will begin weekly auctions of foreign currency to commercial banks and implement stricter oversight of licensed currency exchange bureaus.

The move comes after a period of intense volatility in the parallel exchange market, which has driven up the cost of imported fuel, wheat, and medicines. The central bank has also capped the daily amount of local currency that can be transferred via mobile banking apps to curb speculative trading.

'Our priority is to restore order to the foreign exchange market and curb inflationary pressures that impact everyday citizens,' said the Governor of the Central Bank in a press briefing. Commercial banks have welcomed the foreign currency auctions, stating they will help meet the backlog of import requests from local manufacturers.
author: Zeinab Salih

title: Heavy Rainfall in Eastern Sudan Triggers Preventive Flood Alerts
excerpt: Meteorologists warn of flash floods in Kassala and Gedaref states, urging residents near seasonal rivers to evacuate to higher ground.
content:
KASSALA — The Sudan Meteorological Authority has issued an amber warning for Kassala and Gedaref states after satellite imagery showed a major storm system moving across the Ethiopian highlands. Heavy rains are expected to trigger sudden flash floods along the Gash and Atbara rivers, threatening seasonal agricultural settlements.

Local civil defense committees in Kassala have begun placing sandbags along low-lying river bends and establishing emergency shelters in public school buildings. Public radio broadcasts are advising families in high-risk zones to pack essential documents and relocate to designated high grounds.

'The Gash River is known for its sudden and destructive torrents,' warned regional emergency coordinator Tariq Osman. 'We are working closely with village leaders to ensure early warning messages are spread by megaphone in places without mobile phone coverage.' Aid agencies have prepositioned emergency shelter kits in the area.
author: Tariq Osman

title: Sudan and South Sudan Agree to Resume Joint Oil Pipeline Maintenance
excerpt: Technical teams will cooperate to repair damaged pumping stations along the oil pipeline terminating at Port Sudan.
content:
JUBA — In a joint press conference, the ministries of petroleum of Sudan and South Sudan announced a technical agreement to resume maintenance on the pipeline that carries South Sudanese crude oil to terminals on the Red Sea coast. Pumping operations had been partially suspended due to mechanical failures at key pressure stations.

Under the agreement, a joint team of engineers will be granted safe passage and logistical support to replace damaged valves and valves at the pumping stations. South Sudan’s government will provide technical parts, while Sudan will oversee the security and maintenance of the coastal storage facilities.

'This pipeline is a vital financial lifeline for both Juba and Khartoum,' remarked energy analyst Mohamed El-Fatih. 'Resuming full flows will inject much-needed foreign currency reserves into both economies and stabilize domestic refinery operations.' Repair works are slated to begin next week.
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: African Union High-Level Delegation Arrives in Khartoum
excerpt: The diplomatic team aims to meet with diverse political factions to support a comprehensive national peace framework.
content:
KHARTOUM — A delegation of senior diplomats representing the African Union Peace and Security Council has landed in Khartoum. The delegation is tasked with hosting consultative meetings with civil society groups, political parties, and traditional leaders to lay the groundwork for an inclusive national dialogue.

The visit comes amid renewed regional diplomatic efforts to bring lasting stability to Sudan. The AU delegates plan to focus on security guarantees, humanitarian access, and a timeline for transitioning to a civilian-led government. They will also visit regional centers, including Port Sudan and Wad Madani.

'The African Union stands in solidarity with the Sudanese people in their quest for peace and democracy,' said the head of the delegation during an arrival statement. 'We are here to listen and facilitate a process that is entirely Sudanese-led and Sudanese-owned.' The delegation's tour is expected to last five days.
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: Sudanese Red Crescent Boosts Water Purification Systems in IDP Camps
excerpt: New solar-powered water filtration units have been installed in Gedaref state camps to prevent waterborne illnesses.
content:
GEDAREF — The Sudanese Red Crescent Society, in partnership with international water sanitation agencies, has installed ten new solar-powered water purification units at displacement camps in Gedaref State. The systems will provide clean drinking water to over 25,000 displaced persons who fled ongoing regional instability.

The solar units draw water from local wells, filtering out sediment and biological contaminants without requiring diesel generators. This sustainable setup reduces operating costs and ensures a reliable water supply during fuel shortages.

'Clean water is our primary defense against cholera and other waterborne diseases,' explained field coordinator Fatima Al-Nour. 'These solar filtration systems represent a sustainable, eco-friendly solution that will serve the community for years to come.' Local camp committees have been trained to maintain the panels and filter membranes.
author: Fatima Al-Nour

title: East African Trade Bloc Welcomes Sudan's Infrastructure Integration Plan
excerpt: Sudan's proposals to link railway networks and electricity grids with regional partners receive strong endorsement.
content:
ADDIS ABABA — Ministers from the Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD) have formally endorsed Sudan's regional infrastructure integration master plan. The ambitious plan proposes linking Sudan's railway network with Ethiopia and expanding high-voltage transmission lines to export surplus solar energy.

The regional trade bloc agreed that transport and energy integration are critical to unlocking East Africa's economic potential. A joint steering committee will be established next month to draft feasibility studies for the rail link between Port Sudan and Addis Ababa.

'A connected East Africa is a resilient East Africa,' remarked regional development specialist Amna El-Sadiq. 'By linking our rail networks, we cut transit costs by up to forty percent and make our regional products competitive on the global market.' Funding partners have indicated interest in financing the pre-engineering designs.
author: Amna El-Sadiq

title: Renewable Energy Drive: Solar Irrigation Transforms Northern State Farms
excerpt: Farmers along the Nile are phasing out diesel pumps in favor of solar-powered tube wells, reducing farming overheads.
content:
DONGOLA — In the agricultural lands of Northern State, a quiet green revolution is taking place. Farmers are rapidly replacing expensive and high-maintenance diesel water pumps with solar-powered irrigation systems, drastically reducing production costs and increasing crop yields.

The initiative, supported by local agricultural cooperatives, has installed over 500 solar pump packages this year alone. The solar panels provide reliable, free energy during daylight hours, allowing farmers to irrigate wheat, dates, and alfalfa fields at optimal times without worrying about diesel shortages.

'With diesel prices soaring, farming was becoming unprofitable,' said Dongola wheat farmer Osman Idris. 'Solar power has cut my monthly operating expenses by seventy percent. Now, I can afford to cultivate all of my land and hire more local workers.' Cooperative leaders hope to establish a local assembly plant for solar panels to further reduce setup costs.
author: Tariq Osman

title: The Musical Heritage of Omdurman: Sufi Chants and Contemporary Folk
excerpt: A new cultural archive project is digitizing rare musical recordings to preserve Sudan's diverse vocal and instrumental history.
content:
OMDURMAN — A group of young cultural researchers has launched a digitization project to document Sudan's musical history. Based in Omdurman, the team is transferring analog tape reels and vinyl records containing traditional Sufi chants and early 20th-century urban folk music into high-fidelity digital archives.

The project aims to save rare recordings by early Sudanese pioneers who blended traditional oud instrumentation with modern string sections. The researchers are also filming oral history interviews with elderly musicians in regional capitals to document the origins of traditional scales and rhythms.

'Our music is the narrative of our shared identity,' said archive director Fatima Al-Nour. 'By digitizing these recordings, we make them accessible to young musicians who are looking to reconnect with their roots and create contemporary Sudanese music that honors our past.' The digital archive will be launched online as a free educational resource next winter.
author: Fatima Al-Nour

title: Sudanese National Athletics Team Begins Training for African Championships
excerpt: Sudan's top track and field athletes are gathering at a training camp in Kassala to prepare for the upcoming continental games.
content:
KASSALA — Sudan's national athletics team has established a high-altitude training camp in the picturesque foothills of Kassala. The squad, consisting of 24 runners, jumpers, and throwers, is preparing for the African Athletics Championships scheduled to take place in Nairobi later this year.

The training regimen focuses on building endurance and refining technique under the supervision of local and regional coaches. Among the squad are several promising middle-distance runners who have recently posted competitive times in regional meets.

'Our athletes have shown incredible dedication, training under very basic conditions,' said team manager Hassan Bashir. 'Kassala’s climate and terrain provide an excellent environment for building physical endurance. We are confident that Sudan will bring home medals, particularly in the 800m and 1500m events.' The national Olympic committee has secured sponsorships to cover travel and equipment costs.
author: Hassan Bashir

title: Sudan's Universities Form Consortium to Support Displaced Students
excerpt: A group of ten public and private universities has agreed to a credit-transfer system to help students complete their degrees.
content:
PORT SUDAN — In a major effort to save the academic futures of thousands of young people, ten leading Sudanese universities have signed a cooperative agreement to form the Academic Continuity Consortium. The agreement establishes a unified credit-transfer system, allowing students displaced from Khartoum to enroll in campuses in safer eastern and northern states.

The consortium will also share online libraries and digital lecture halls, enabling professors to deliver lectures to students scattered across the country. Additionally, participating universities will offer tuition discounts and flexible payment plans to displaced families.

'We cannot allow a generation of Sudanese youth to lose their higher education,' stated Dr. Amna El-Sadiq, vice-chancellor of one of the participating institutions. 'This consortium ensures that a student's hard work in Khartoum is recognized and they can graduate on time, ready to contribute to post-crisis reconstruction.'
author: Amna El-Sadiq

title: Red Sea Shipping Lines Resume Regular Feeder Services to Port Sudan
excerpt: Three major global shipping container lines have announced the resumption of weekly scheduled arrivals at the south port.
content:
PORT SUDAN — In a boost for the nation’s commercial sector, three international shipping lines have announced they are resuming regular weekly container feeder services to the Port Sudan southern terminal. The decision reflects growing confidence in the port's upgraded cargo handling capacities and streamlined security procedures.

The resumption of scheduled services will help stabilize maritime freight rates and ensure a steady supply of raw materials for domestic manufacturers. It will also reduce transit times for agricultural exporters shipping sesame and livestock to Gulf markets.

'Having reliable, scheduled container services is critical for business planning,' remarked local chamber of commerce member Ibrahim Nour. 'It means we can import spare parts and export agricultural crops with predictable timelines, which will help lower the overall cost of doing business in Sudan.' Port authorities are adding extra shifts to handle the anticipated increase in container volumes.
author: Zeinab Salih

title: Sudan's Software Engineers Build Offline Medical Directory App
excerpt: Volunteers have developed an android application that lists open clinics, pharmacies, and emergency rooms without needing data.
content:
OMDURMAN — A volunteer team of Sudanese software developers has launched a mobile application called 'Shifa' (Healing) to help citizens locate operating healthcare facilities. The android application functions entirely offline, using compressed databases updated weekly via peer-to-peer sharing or local hotspots.

The app lists coordinates, operational hours, and available medicine stocks for pharmacies, clinics, and emergency response rooms across multiple states. It also includes basic first-aid tutorials and emergency contact numbers for volunteer ambulance drivers.

'When networks are down and pharmacies are closed, finding medicine is a matter of life and death,' explained tech lead Mohamed El-Fatih. 'Our app store matches user locations with the nearest open facility without using any internet data. It’s a community-driven database built to save lives.' The team is working on a version that can run on simple feature phones via USSD codes.
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: Al-Merrikh Signs Promising Young Strikers to Rebuild Championship Squad
excerpt: Sudan's historic Red Devils have announced the signing of three youth international forwards to bolster their attack.
content:
OMDURMAN — Al-Merrikh SC, one of Sudan's most successful and historic football clubs, has announced the signing of three promising young strikers from regional academies. The signings are part of an ambitious multi-year strategy to rebuild the squad and challenge for domestic and continental titles.

The new players, all under the age of 21, have represented Sudan at the Under-20 level and are known for their speed and technical finishing. They will join the first-team squad immediately at their temporary training base in Port Sudan.

'We are building a team for the future,' said Al-Merrikh’s sporting director during a press conference. 'These young men represent the next generation of Sudanese football talent. We believe that with proper coaching and regular playing time, they will become stars of the national team.' The club is also planning a pre-season friendly tour in North Africa.
author: Hassan Bashir

title: The Literary Legacy of Tayeb Salih Inspires New Generation of Writers
excerpt: A creative writing workshop in Port Sudan honors the author of 'Season of Migration to the North' with youth storytelling grants.
content:
PORT SUDAN — The profound influence of Tayeb Salih, Sudan’s most celebrated novelist, was celebrated at a week-long creative writing workshop that concluded yesterday. The event, titled 'Nile Narratives,' brought together thirty aspiring young writers from across the country to study Salih’s complex portrayals of identity, migration, and post-colonial life.

The workshop, organized by a local cultural center, concluded with the announcement of three storytelling grants named in honor of the author. The grants will support the publication of short story collections exploring contemporary Sudanese experiences of displacement and community resilience.

'Tayeb Salih taught us how to tell our own stories with dignity and artistic depth,' remarked workshop mentor Fatima Al-Nour. 'His legacy reminds us that literature is a powerful mirror for society. The stories written by these young participants prove that Sudan’s literary voice remains vibrant and essential.'
author: Fatima Al-Nour

title: Sudan Joins East African Common Market Payment System Alignment Initiative
excerpt: The central bank will integrate its clearing house with COMESA's payment portal to facilitate faster regional trade settlements.
content:
LUSAKA — In a significant step toward regional economic integration, the Central Bank of Sudan has signed an agreement to join the Common Market for Eastern and Southern Africa (COMESA) Regional Payment and Settlement System (REPSS). The integration will allow Sudanese commercial banks to settle cross-border transactions in local currencies, bypassing expensive intermediary banks.

The system aims to slash the time and cost of processing trade payments between Sudan and its main East African trading partners, including Kenya and Uganda. It is expected to benefit small-and-medium enterprises engaged in cross-border agriculture and manufacturing trade.

'By joining REPSS, we are removing a major barrier to regional trade,' said Sudan's diplomatic envoy. 'It will enable our exporters to receive payments within hours rather than days, improving cash flows and strengthening regional economic partnerships.' Integration is scheduled to be completed by late autumn.
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: Gezira Scheme Expands Sunflower Cultivation to Diversify Oil Seed Production
excerpt: Farmers plant over 50,000 acres of sunflowers, aiming to reduce dependence on imported cooking oil and boost local incomes.
content:
WAD MADANI — Sudan's historic Gezira Scheme, one of the world's largest irrigation networks, has launched a major crop diversification campaign by planting over 50,000 acres of sunflowers. The initiative aims to boost domestic production of vegetable cooking oils, reducing the country's reliance on costly imports.

Sunflower cultivation offers several advantages: the crop is drought-tolerant, requires fewer fertilizers than wheat or cotton, and has a shorter maturity cycle. Local crushing factories in Wad Madani have upgraded their machinery to process the sunflower seeds into cooking oil and high-protein animal feed.

'This is a strategic move to secure our domestic food supply,' explained agriculture expert Ibrahim Nour. 'By producing our own vegetable oil, we save foreign currency and create jobs in the local processing sector. The farmers are enthusiastic because sunflowers offer high profit margins.' The harvest is expected to yield over 40,000 metric tons of oil seed.
author: Zeinab Salih

title: Port Sudan Desalination Plant Undergoes Urgent Technical Maintenance
excerpt: Engineers work to restore the city's primary drinking water facility, aiming to return output to its full daily capacity.
content:
PORT SUDAN — The Red Sea State Water Corporation has deployed emergency technical crews to perform critical repairs at the main seawater desalination plant in Port Sudan. The facility, which provides over forty percent of the coastal city’s drinking water, had been operating at half capacity due to filter blockages.

The maintenance work involves replacing reverse osmosis membranes and servicing the high-pressure intake pumps. The drop in water production has caused water shortages in several residential districts, prompting authorities to distribute water via tanker trucks.

'Restoring the desalination plant is our top municipal priority,' stated emergency director Tariq Osman. 'Our technicians are working round-the-clock to replace the damaged filters. We expect the plant to resume full operations and restore normal water pressures by Tuesday.' Aid organizations are assisting with logistics and spare parts supply.
author: Tariq Osman

title: Sudan's Tech Hubs Collaborate to Host First Virtual Hackathon
excerpt: Over 200 developers competed online to build civic tools for healthcare, education, and mutual aid distribution.
content:
PORT SUDAN — In a display of digital solidarity, four Sudanese technology hubs joined forces to organize the nation's first virtual coding hackathon. The 48-hour event brought together more than 200 developers, designers, and project managers from various states and the diaspora to build software solutions for real-world civic challenges.

The winning projects focused on optimizing supply chains for emergency response rooms, creating SMS-based maternal health trackers, and cataloging local solar panel repair technicians. Mentors from international technology companies provided guidance and technical support throughout the competition.

'The talent in Sudan's tech ecosystem is incredible,' said coordinator Fatima Al-Nour. 'Despite internet blackouts and power cuts, these young developers built working prototypes that address real community needs. We are now working with regional incubation funds to help them turn these projects into viable startups.'
author: Fatima Al-Nour

title: Traditional Wrestling Championships Draw Record Crowds in Kassala
excerpt: Athletes from the Nuba Mountains and eastern states gathered to compete in Sudan's historic wrestling games.
content:
KASSALA — Thousands of spectators gathered at the Kassala municipal stadium to witness the annual Sudan Traditional Wrestling Championships. The tournament, which has deep roots in the cultural heritage of the Nuba Mountains, featured over 60 wrestlers representing various clubs and regional states.

The matches, characterized by rapid grappling and strength, were accompanied by traditional drumming and singing, creating an electric atmosphere. The championship aims to celebrate Sudanese heritage and foster social unity through sport.

'Wrestling is more than a sport; it is a celebration of our history and a bridge between different cultures in Sudan,' remarked tournament coordinator Hassan Bashir. 'Seeing Nuba wrestlers compete in Kassala before a diverse crowd shows the power of sports to bring people together.' The championship concluded with the crowning of a new heavyweight champion from South Kordofan.
author: Hassan Bashir

title: Contemporary Sudanese Painters Showcase Work in Cairo Gallery
excerpt: An exhibition featuring fifteen Sudanese artists highlights the themes of homeland, memory, and hope in exile.
content:
CAIRO — An art exhibition titled 'Colors of the Nile' has opened at a prominent gallery in Cairo, showcasing the works of fifteen contemporary Sudanese painters. The exhibition, which will run for two weeks, explores complex themes of displacement, the search for home, and hope for a peaceful future.

The paintings use a variety of media, including acrylics on canvas, calligraphy, and mixed-media collages incorporating traditional Sudanese textiles and soil from the banks of the Nile. The opening night drew art critics, diplomats, and members of the Sudanese community in Egypt.

'Art allows us to express emotions that words cannot capture,' explained exhibiting artist Amna El-Sadiq. 'Through these canvases, we show the world that despite the pain of leaving our homes, our culture and spirit remain unbroken. We are painting our memories and our dreams for a rebuilt Sudan.' A portion of the proceeds from art sales will support art therapy programs for displaced children.
author: Amna El-Sadiq

title: Sudanese Chambers of Commerce Form Advisory Committee on Trade Barriers
excerpt: The new committee will draft policy recommendations to ease border customs and simplify banking regulations.
content:
PORT SUDAN — The Federation of Sudanese Chambers of Commerce has established a high-level Advisory Committee on Trade Barriers. The committee, comprising business leaders and trade lawyers, will draft policy proposals to simplify customs processes, eliminate double taxation, and coordinate with international banks to ease trade finance.

The initiative comes as local businesses struggle with cash flow issues and high transit tariffs. The committee’s first report, scheduled to be submitted to the ministry of finance next month, will focus on easing transit bottlenecks along the Port Sudan-Kassala trade route.

'We need a regulatory environment that supports business survival and expansion,' said committee chairman Salah Ahmed. 'By presenting unified, pragmatic solutions to the authorities, we hope to reduce the cost of trading and make essential commodities more affordable for ordinary citizens.'
author: Amna El-Sadiq

title: Sudanese Livestock Exporters Secure New Veterinary Certification Deal
excerpt: A deal with Middle Eastern import regulators ensures streamlined health screenings for sheep exports at Port Sudan.
content:
PORT SUDAN — Sudan's livestock export sector, a major source of foreign exchange, has received a significant boost with the signing of a new veterinary quarantine agreement with Gulf import authorities. The deal streamlines health screening protocols at the specialized veterinary quarantine facilities in Port Sudan.

Under the new framework, livestock shipments will undergo standardized vaccinations and health screenings, with digital health certificates issued directly at the port. This reduces the quarantine period from ten days to forty-eight hours, lowering feed costs for exporters and reducing livestock mortality rates.

'This agreement is a testament to the high standards of our veterinary authorities,' remarked agricultural specialist Ibrahim Nour. 'It restores buyer confidence and ensures that Sudanese sheep and cattle, which are highly prized in Gulf markets, can be exported efficiently. We expect export volumes to increase by twenty percent in the coming season.'
author: Zeinab Salih

title: Community Kitchens in Omdurman Receive Emergency Grain Shipment
excerpt: Local emergency response rooms distribute millet and wheat to support neighborhood communal dining halls.
content:
OMDURMAN — A network of neighborhood-run community kitchens in Omdurman has received over 120 metric tons of millet and wheat flour. The shipment, procured by local charity organizations and donor funds, will supply kitchen operations that provide free daily hot meals to families facing food insecurity.

These kitchens, staffed by volunteer youth from local Emergency Response Rooms, prepare large pots of traditional porridge and stew, serving thousands of meals daily. The arrival of the grain supply ensures that thirty kitchens can continue operating for at least another month.

'These communal kitchens are a crucial survival mechanism,' explained aid volunteer Yassir Abdullah. 'They are run by the community, for the community, showing the incredible solidarity of Omdurman's residents. Our challenge is to ensure a steady supply of cooking gas and clean water to keep the pots boiling.'
author: Yassir Abdullah

title: Mobile Internet Speeds Improve as Telecoms Expand Fiber Connections
excerpt: Telecommunications providers upgrade base stations along the Red Sea coast, boosting digital connectivity for businesses.
content:
PORT SUDAN — Telecommunications operators in Port Sudan have completed a series of network upgrades, linking local base stations to the newly laid fiber-optic submarine cables. The upgrades have resulted in a significant boost to mobile internet speeds and network stability in the coastal business district.

The improved connectivity is expected to benefit commercial banks, shipping logistics firms, and government agencies that rely on real-time data transfers. Operators are also deploying new cellular towers to cover expanding residential areas on the city’s outskirts.

'Reliable high-speed internet is essential for any modern economy,' said IT consultant Mohamed El-Fatih. 'These fiber upgrades will improve online banking operations and allow our local tech startups to develop complex cloud-based services. We hope to see similar fiber extensions rolled out to Kassala and Gedaref soon.'
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: Sudanese Table Tennis Team Clinches Bronze in Regional Tournament
excerpt: Sudan's doubles team defeated East African opponents in a thrilling playoff match, securing a podium finish.
content:
ASMARA — Sudan's national table tennis doubles team has secured a bronze medal at the East African Table Tennis Championship. The duo, who trained in Kassala, defeated their regional opponents in a thrilling five-set playoff match that showcased their tactical reflexes and teamwork.

After losing the first two sets, the Sudanese players mounted a dramatic comeback, adjusting their serve returns and utilizing aggressive spin shots. The final set was a tense affair, with Sudan eventually clinching victory with a 12-10 scoreline.

'This medal is a massive milestone for table tennis in Sudan,' said coach Hassan Bashir. 'Our players had very limited training facilities, but their passion and mental focus were unmatched. This victory will inspire more young people to take up the sport and shows that Sudan can compete at the highest levels.'
author: Hassan Bashir

title: Traditional Crafts Exhibition in Kassala Highlights Women Cooperatives
excerpt: The week-long market showcased handwoven carpets, leather goods, and pottery made by displaced women craft collectives.
content:
KASSALA — A colorful exhibition celebrating Sudan's rich handicraft traditions has opened in Kassala, highlighting the work of women's cooperatives. The exhibition features handwoven colorful straw baskets, leather sandals, beaded jewelry, and pottery produced by displaced women who have established craft collectives.

The cooperatives provide members with training, raw materials, and access to collective markets, allowing them to earn an independent income. Visitors to the exhibition included local residents, aid workers, and collectors seeking authentic Sudanese art.

'Crafting is not just a source of income; it is a way to preserve our cultural stories,' explained cooperative leader Fatima Al-Nour. 'Every pattern in these baskets represents a traditional design from our villages. By selling these crafts, we support our families and keep our traditions alive.' A digital platform is being developed to market the crafts internationally.
author: Fatima Al-Nour

title: Sudanese Envoys Hold Talks with UN Envoy to Discuss Humanitarian Corridors
excerpt: Negotiators focus on securing safe passage for emergency food convoys and medical teams in remote regions.
content:
GENEVA — A delegation of Sudanese diplomats and humanitarian officials has concluded a round of formal talks with the United Nations Special Envoy for Sudan. The discussions focused on establishing permanent humanitarian corridors to facilitate the delivery of food, vaccines, and shelter materials to vulnerable populations.

The delegates agreed on the need to set up designated de-confliction zones along main highway routes and simplify administrative clearances for aid agencies. The UN has committed to expanding its logistical support, including providing monitoring vehicles and communication gear.

'Ensuring aid reaches those in need is a moral and logistical priority,' said the lead diplomat. 'These talks have established a clear, practical framework to protect aid workers and ensure convoys can travel without delay. We urge all regional partners to support this humanitarian effort.'
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: Sesame Crop Yields Rise in Gedaref as Improved Seeds Roll Out
excerpt: The introduction of drought-resistant white sesame seeds helps farmers achieve record yields in eastern agricultural belt.
content:
GEDAREF — Farmers in Sudan’s prime sesame-producing region, Gedaref State, are celebrating a successful harvest, with crop yields rising by fifteen percent. The boost in production is largely attributed to the widespread distribution of improved, drought-resistant white sesame seed varieties by local agricultural research centers.

White sesame is highly sought after by international tahini and oil processors, offering premium prices on the global market. The new seed varieties also boast higher resistance to common pests and require less water during the critical flowering stage.

'These new seeds have made a dramatic difference,' said Gedaref farmer Ibrahim Nour. 'Despite late rains, the sesame crop was robust and of excellent quality. The premium price we receive will allow us to reinvest in modern harvesting equipment for the next season.' Exporters in Port Sudan are preparing for high-volume shipments.
author: Zeinab Salih

title: Eastern Sudan Health Board Initiates Clean Water Awareness Campaign
excerpt: Volunteers distribute chlorine tablets and educational leaflets to prevent seasonal cholera outbreaks.
content:
KASSALA — In preparation for the upcoming summer rains, the Eastern Sudan Health Board has launched a preemptive clean water and sanitation awareness campaign. The initiative, titled 'Safe Water, Safe Families,' aims to educate local communities on simple methods to treat drinking water and prevent waterborne disease outbreaks.

Volunteers are visiting homes and local markets, distributing water purification tablets, clean storage jerrycans, and educational leaflets written in local languages. They are also conducting community workshops demonstrating how to build simple handwashing stations using local materials.

'Preventative education is our most cost-effective healthcare tool,' said health officer Tariq Osman. 'By teaching families how to purify their water and maintain basic hygiene, we can prevent seasonal outbreaks before they start. The cooperation from local neighborhood leaders has been key to our reach.' The campaign aims to reach 50,000 households.
author: Tariq Osman

title: Solar-Powered Water Pumping Station Commissioned in River Nile State
excerpt: The new community station will provide clean agricultural irrigation and drinking water for three rural villages.
content:
SHENDI — A large-scale solar-powered water pumping station has been commissioned in River Nile State, providing a clean and reliable source of irrigation and drinking water for three agricultural villages. The facility, which features a 120-panel solar array, was built by local engineering contractors.

The station pumps water from a deep underground aquifer into a elevated 50,000-liter storage tank, distributing it to agricultural fields and domestic taps. The system replaces diesel-powered pumps that had been unreliable due to high fuel costs and mechanical breakdowns.

'This solar station provides our community with water security,' said local leader Salah Ahmed. 'Our fields are now irrigated consistently, and our children have access to clean drinking water near our homes. It is a modern solution that has transformed our daily lives.' The project was funded by a partnership of local farmers and development organizations.
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: Sudanese Chess Federation Hosts National Youth Championship
excerpt: Over 80 young players competed in Port Sudan, showcasing high-level strategic play in Swiss-system tournament.
content:
PORT SUDAN — The Sudanese Chess Federation has hosted its annual National Youth Chess Championship, bringing together over 80 young players from various regional states. The tournament, played under the standard FIDE Swiss-system rules, aimed to identify promising talent for the national junior team.

The competition was fierce, with several matches extending to dramatic endgame scenarios. The championship was divided into age categories from Under-12 to Under-18, with trophies and academic scholarships awarded to the top players.

'Chess teaches patience, logic, and long-term planning,' said tournament director Hassan Bashir. 'The level of play we witnessed from these young minds is outstanding. Despite limited coaching materials, their strategic depth is impressive. We hope to send our category champions to the African Junior Championships next spring.'
author: Hassan Bashir

title: Sudan's Archaeological Excavations Reveal Ancient Iron Smelting Sites
excerpt: Discoveries near the ancient city of Meroe shed light on the advanced industrial history of the Kingdom of Kush.
content:
MEROE — Archaeological excavations near the ancient ruins of Meroe have uncovered three previously undocumented iron smelting furnace sites dating back to the Kingdom of Kush. The discoveries include intact slag deposits, charcoal pits, and clay tuyeres (blowpipes) used to pump air into the furnaces.

Meroe was renowned in the ancient world as the 'Birmingham of Africa' due to its massive iron production capacity. The newly discovered sites suggest that the scale of Kushite iron working was even more widespread than previously estimated, operating as a centralized industrial network.

'These furnaces represent the cutting edge of ancient metallurgy,' explained lead researcher Dr. Amna El-Sadiq. 'The Kushites developed specialized clay mixtures for their furnaces to withstand extreme temperatures, producing high-grade steel that was traded across North Africa. It is a testament to the industrial genius of our ancestors.'
author: Amna El-Sadiq

title: Sudan and Kenya Sign Joint Trade Promotion Pact for Tea and Spices
excerpt: The bilateral agreement reduces tariffs and establishes direct shipping lines between Mombasa and Port Sudan.
content:
NAIROBI — In a move to boost regional trade, the governments of Sudan and Kenya have signed a comprehensive Trade Promotion Pact. The agreement focus on reducing import tariffs on Kenyan tea and streamlining health inspection standards for Sudanese spices and gum arabic exported to Kenya.

The pact also establishes a joint logistics working group to coordinate direct container shipping schedules between the ports of Mombasa and Port Sudan, bypassing regional transshipment hubs to cut transit times.

'Sudan is one of the largest consumers of Kenyan tea, while Kenya offers a growing market for our specialty agricultural crops,' said Sudan's trade minister during the signing ceremony. 'This agreement removes administrative bottlenecks and makes trade faster and more profitable for exporters in both nations.'
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: Red Sea Gold Mining Companies Commit to Environmental Safety Guidelines
excerpt: A coalition of mining firms signs a pledge to phase out mercury use and fund local water monitoring systems.
content:
PORT SUDAN — A coalition of gold mining companies operating in the Red Sea hills has signed a voluntary Environmental Safety Pledge, committing to stricter ecological safeguards. The signatory firms have pledged to phase out the use of mercury and cyanide in gold extraction within the next eighteen months.

Instead, companies will transition to modern, chemical-free gravity concentration systems. The pledge also includes commitments to fund independent, monthly water quality testing in wells surrounding mining concessions and restore topsoil in abandoned excavation areas.

'Sustainable mining is critical for the long-term health of our communities and environment,' noted resource economist Ibrahim Nour. 'Mercury contamination poses a severe threat to local aquifers and grazing lands. We welcome this industry commitment and will monitor their compliance closely.' The mining sector is a major source of employment in the area.
author: Zeinab Salih

title: Emergency Response Rooms Launch Winter Blanket Distribution in Kassala
excerpt: Volunteers distribute warm bedding and thermal wear to thousands of displaced families in temporary camps.
content:
KASSALA — Anticipating a sharp drop in winter temperatures, local volunteer-run Emergency Response Rooms (ERRs) have launched a winterization distribution drive in Kassala State. The campaign, titled 'Warmth for All,' aims to distribute warm blankets, sleeping mats, and winter jackets to families living in makeshift tents and school buildings.

The distribution is organized by neighborhood youth groups who maintain census records of displaced families to ensure aid is allocated fairly. The supplies were purchased with donations from community members and local businesses.

'Many of these families fled their homes with only the clothes on their backs,' said volunteer coordinator Yassir Abdullah. 'Winter in Kassala can be harsh, especially for young children and the elderly living in drafts. These blankets are a basic but critical intervention to protect health and dignity.' The group hopes to secure additional funding to purchase heating fuel.
author: Yassir Abdullah

title: Sudanese Fintech App Launches Mobile micro-loans for Small Merchants
excerpt: The new digital loan feature allows registered market traders to borrow interest-free inventory capital.
content:
PORT SUDAN — A leading Sudanese mobile payment startup has introduced a new digital micro-loan feature called 'Taysir' (Facilitation), designed to help small market traders finance inventory purchases. The app utilizes transaction histories to assess creditworthiness, bypassing traditional collateral requirements.

The loans are interest-free and structured as short-term trade finance, with repayment terms aligned with weekly market cycles. Registered merchants can request and receive funds within minutes via their mobile phones, allowing them to purchase inventory from wholesalers during high-demand periods.

'Small merchants represent the backbone of our local markets, but they have no access to traditional bank loans,' explained software product manager Mohamed El-Fatih. 'Taysir provides them with instant, accessible capital based on their actual sales data. It’s a powerful tool to keep local trade flowing.' Over 2,000 traders have registered for the pilot program.
author: Mohamed El-Fatih

title: Sudan National Basketball Team Reaches Regional Zone V Finals
excerpt: A thrilling 78-74 win over rivals in the semi-finals guarantees Sudan a slot in the upcoming AfroBasket tournament.
content:
KAMPALA — The Sudanese national basketball team has advanced to the finals of the FIBA Africa Zone V Championship with a thrilling 78-74 victory over their regional rivals. The victory also guarantees the squad a slot in the prestigious AfroBasket tournament next year.

The match was a high-tempo affair, with both teams trading leads throughout the four quarters. Sudan's victory was sealed in the final minute with two clutch three-pointers and solid defensive rebounding from their star center.

'This team has shown incredible heart and character,' remarked assistant coach Hassan Bashir. 'They trained under difficult circumstances, with limited court access and travel disruptions. Reaching the finals and qualifying for AfroBasket is a massive achievement that proves the potential of basketball in Sudan.' The team will face Egypt in the final tomorrow.
author: Hassan Bashir

title: Contemporary Sudanese Writers Discuss National Identity in London Book Fair
excerpt: A panel of three Sudanese authors explored themes of translation, memory, and writing in times of national crisis.
content:
LONDON — A panel discussion titled 'Voices of Sudan' drew a packed audience at the London Book Fair, featuring three prominent contemporary Sudanese writers. The authors discussed how they navigate the themes of translation, exile, and the role of literature in documenting their homeland’s current challenges.

The discussion highlighted how literature can bridge cultural gaps and provide a nuanced, humanized perspective of Sudan that goes beyond headline news. The writers also read extracts from their latest translated works, showcasing the poetic depth of Sudanese Arabic writing.

'Writing in exile is a way of preserving our homes in the country of language,' remarked panelist Fatima Al-Nour. 'We carry the sounds, colors, and memories of the Nile with us, and we write to keep those memories alive for our children. Our stories show that Sudan is a land of deep culture and enduring resilience.'
author: Fatima Al-Nour

title: Sudan and Eritrea Agree to Facilitate Cross-Border Transit Trade
excerpt: A bilateral protocol simplifies customs checks at the Kassala border crossings, boosting local merchant activity.
content:
KASSALA — Delegations from Sudan and Eritrea have concluded bilateral talks in Kassala by signing a joint Border Trade Protocol. The agreement simplifies customs procedures and reduces tariffs on agricultural goods and consumer products traded across the Kassala border crossings.

The protocol also establishes a joint committee to coordinate border security and streamline clearing processes, facilitating faster travel for merchants. Local trade fairs will be held in Kassala and Teseney to promote border merchant partnerships.

'This agreement is a vital economic catalyst for Kassala State,' said local merchant coordinator Salah Ahmed. 'By simplifying border trade, we create jobs, lower the cost of basic consumer goods, and support local farming communities who sell their produce across the border. It’s a practical win for both populations.'
author: Mohamed El-Fatih
